response.description_long:
When a restaurant closes or renovates, its equipment traditionally moves through auctioneers and local dealers at steep discounts — or sits in storage. Buyers, meanwhile, face a used market with no condition standards and no delivery. Forkfleet is the marketplace for used restaurant equipment that puts both sides on one platform, solving a market that has always been big and broken.

Forkfleet is where restaurants buy and sell commercial kitchen equipment: ovens, ranges, refrigeration, prep tables, espresso machines, and more. Every listing is inspected, priced against market data, and shipped door to door. Sellers photograph equipment in the Forkfleet app and get a price estimate from comparable sales. An inspector verifies condition — burners, compressors, door seals, temperature calibration — before the listing goes live with a graded condition report. Buyers see the report, financing options, and a delivery quote. Forkfleet coordinates freight through partner carriers, including liftgate delivery and old-equipment haul-away.

The inspection standard is central to the platform. Inspectors work a written protocol per category: ranges get every burner fired and the oven cycled to temperature; refrigeration gets compressor draw measured and pull-down time logged; espresso machines get pump pressure and boiler checks. The protocol is public, and each checkpoint is photographed. Equipment that fails inspection is either re-priced as Grade C with the issue disclosed or declined. Listings carry a graded condition report — Grade A means inspected with no functional issues; Grade B means cosmetic wear, fully functional; Grade C means a disclosed functional issue, priced accordingly, with the defect documented. Every purchase carries a 30-day working guarantee; if a piece fails within 30 days, Forkfleet arranges repair, replacement, or refund.

Forkfleet charges sellers a commission of 8 percent per sale and buyers a flat service fee; financing is offered through a lending partner. Nothing is consigned to a warehouse — equipment ships directly from seller to buyer. The pricing engine is built on completed Forkfleet sales: model, age, grade, market, and season. A Grade B convection oven in Chicago prices against what Grade B convection ovens actually sold for in Chicago in the last two quarters — not against a dealer's asking price. Sellers see the estimate range and the count of comparable sales behind it; the listing price is the seller's choice, but the estimate is where offers converge.

Founded in Chicago in 2019 by Nate Kowalski and Maria Reyes, Forkfleet is 45 people and has expanded to 12 metro markets: Chicago, Milwaukee, Minneapolis, Detroit, Indianapolis, Columbus, Cleveland, St. Louis, Kansas City, Nashville, Atlanta, and Dallas. It lists over 5,000 items at any time. The founding insight: the used-equipment market did not need another listing site — it needed condition standards and freight, the two things neither side could supply alone. Licensed dealers list on Forkfleet under the same inspection standard; about a fifth of listings come from dealers. The guarantee claims rate is published on the trust page — Grade B refrigeration, the highest-volume category, runs under 3 percent.
